participants.reasoning: The extracted description matches the official study setting, age range, enrollment context, and follow-up subgroup details, with the only difference being that it reports 940 enrolled children for the broader analysis while the official baseline table lists 609 with group counts, which is not contradictory to the study description.
participants.extracted: The study enrolled 940 preschool children aged 6–35 months from an urban slum in New Delhi, India, who presented to a community-based clinic with acute diarrhoea at baseline. Children were randomized to zinc supplementation or control; for the prevalence analysis, all 940 enrolled children were included. For prospective morbidity analyses, a subset of 116 control-group children in the lower and upper baseline plasma zinc quartiles was followed for 120 days, and 193 control-group children without diarrhoea in the 3 days before the 120-day blood sample were used for comparison of zinc deficiency prevalence after follow-up.
participants.official:
Country: India; setting: Kalkaji, New Delhi; urbanicity: urban
Inclusion criteria: reported passage of at least 4 unformed stools in the previous 24 h; a diarrheal duration of < 7 d; permanent residence in the Kalkaji area
Exclusion criteria: malnutrition judged clinically to be sufficiently severe to require hospitalization
Baseline characteristics
Avg age (months): 16; min age (months): 6; max age (months): 35; % female: 47.7
Avg height‐for‐age z score: N/A; stunting: both ‐ separate data not

interventions.reasoning: The extracted text describes zinc given to children with acute diarrhoea versus placebo/waiting list control, while the official intervention specifies a 6-month daily zinc gluconate solution with multiple micronutrients, which does not match the acute diarrhoea supplementation context.
interventions.extracted: Zinc supplementation was evaluated in a double-masked randomized controlled trial. Children aged 6–35 months with acute diarrhoea were randomly allocated to receive either zinc or control treatment, with the control group described as placebo/waiting list control in the systematic review PICO. Supplementation was given during follow-up to assess its effects on diarrhoea, dysentery, and respiratory morbidity.
interventions.official:
Group 1:zinc
Formulation: solution; compound: gluconate; frequency: daily; duration (months): 6; dose (mg): 10; co‐intervention(s): multiple micronutrients
Group 2:no zinc
Placebo given; co‐intervention(s): multiple micronutrients

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes align with the official morbidity and zinc-related secondary outcomes, with only minor differences in wording and measurement units.
outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure morbidity outcomes related to infection and gastrointestinal illness over follow-up, including days of illness, days and episodes of diarrhoea, days of dysentery, days with acute lower respiratory tract infection (ALRI), and episodes of pneumonia. These outcomes were assessed prospectively during home-visits every fifth day, with a 120-day follow-up window used for the morbidity analysis. In addition, vomiting in the last 24 hours was recorded at baseline, and baseline and 120-day plasma zinc concentrations were measured to assess zinc status using atomic absorption spectrophotometry (µg/dL).
